data.title: Hong Kong to host Apec finance ministers’ meeting in November 2026
data.description:
Hong Kong will hold the Apec finance ministers’ meeting in November next year as part of China’s efforts to host the regional economic forum, the Post has learned.
A source also revealed on Friday that Macau would host the tourism ministers’ meeting for the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation forum.
Last month, Chinese President Xi Jinping announced that the 33rd Apec economic leaders’ meeting would be held in Shenzhen next year.
He said the country would use its role as forum host to work with other countries to promote growth and prosperity in the Asia-Pacific region.
During the Apec handover ceremony last month, Xi described Shenzhen as a place “adjacent to Hong Kong”, noting it had “transformed from a backward fishing village into a modern international metropolis in just a few decades”.
He also said the Greater Bay Area could be considered a growth pole for the world economy.
The bay area refers to Beijing’s ambitious scheme to integrate Hong Kong, Macau and nine southern Chinese cities into an economic powerhouse.
More to follow …

data.title: Chinese firms slam EU anti-subsidy probes as ‘discriminatory’
data.description:
A Chinese business group has urged the European Union to halt its “discriminatory and disproportionate” use of anti-subsidy probes to target Chinese companies, as Brussels steps up its scrutiny of firms allegedly receiving unfair state subsidies.
The EU launched an in-depth foreign subsidy regulation (FSR) investigation against the Chinese security firm Nuctech on Thursday, just days after Brussels reportedly raided the Chinese-invested e-commerce platform Temu’s European headquarters in Dublin under a separate FSR probe.
In a statement, the China Chamber of Commerce to the EU (CCCEU) expressed “firm opposition” to what it described as the European Commission’s “continued, targeted and excessive” use of investigations against Chinese-invested enterprises.
The body indicated the FSR – a tool introduced by Brussels in 2023 – was being used to unfairly single out China’s firms, as the “majority” of FSR investigations launched so far had involved Chinese or Chinese-invested businesses.
The chamber warned the Commission not to “abuse its investigative power” by interfering in the normal business activities of foreign companies, while arguing that Brussels’ definition of foreign subsidies was too broad and “may significantly exceed reasonable boundaries” under existing international rules.
In January, China’s Ministry of Commerce concluded a six-month investigation into the EU’s FSR enforcement against Chinese enterprises, which found the instrument represented a trade and investment barrier that had caused Chinese firms direct and indirect losses of about €2.1 billion (US$2.46 billion).


The EU’s renewed efforts to probe Chinese companies over their use of financial subsidies come amid rising concerns in the bloc about Europe’s industrial base being unable to compete against an influx of Chinese imports, with French President Emmanuel Macron warning European industry faced a “life or death” moment.
Since FSR entered into force, three out of four in-depth reviews and all of the ex officio investigations initiated by the European Commission have involved Chinese companies, according to a report published in May by Arnold & Porter, an American law firm with offices in China and Europe.
The report also found that Brussels had chosen its ex officio cases in line with its political and economic agenda, focusing on strategic sectors including telecommunications, clean energy, infrastructure and security equipment.
Meanwhile, it had not – as far as publicly known – acted on other instances of alleged unfair competition, such as European professional football clubs’ informal complaints about the financial backing rival clubs received from Qatar and the United Arab Emirates, the report said.
More than 60 per cent of Chinese companies in the EU reported being affected by Brussels’ FSR enforcement actions, with firms citing direct disruptions, lost commercial opportunities and operational risks stemming from ongoing or potential investigations, according to a CCCEU survey of 205 Chinese companies and organisations published this year.
More than half of the respondents said the FSR had caused indirect harm to their commercial reputation and market perception.
Nuctech said it would continue cooperating with the European Commission during its investigation, while adding that it reserved the right to defend its rights and interests.

data.title: Electoral commission head David Lok to lead committee probing deadly Hong Kong fire
data.description:
Judge David Lok Kai-hong, who chairs the Electoral Affairs Commission, will lead an independent review committee set up by the government to investigate the Tai Po fire that killed at least 160 people, Hong Kong’s leader has announced.
Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu said on Friday that the appointed committee members included Legislative Council finance committee chairman Chan Kin-por and outgoing MTR Corporation chairman Rex Auyeung Pak-keun.
The probe is expected to be completed within nine months, with all findings set to be made public.
“The government will fully support the committee’s work, proactively offer information and make full use of each department’s investigative powers to help in gathering evidence,” Lee said.
To ensure the probe is completed within nine months, Lee said the committee would be given flexibility to simplify procedures and apply judicial powers in specific areas of their investigation.
“If the committee deems it necessary to obtain statutory powers on certain issues during the investigation, it may apply to the chief executive to be established as a commission of inquiry, thereby gaining those powers,” he said.
“This method combines flexibility and efficiency while integrating statutory powers and complementing each other so the committee can complete the investigation of broad and complicated issues in nine months.”
Under Cap 86 of the Commissions of Inquiry Ordinance, the inquiry would be deemed a judicial proceeding.
Lok currently serves as the chairman of the Electoral Affairs Commission, a statutory body responsible for making recommendations on constituency boundaries, supervising public elections and regulating the registration of electors.
He was appointed as a magistrate by the Judiciary in 1995 and promoted to judge of the Court of First Instance in 2015.
Lee earlier said the committee would focus on eight areas requiring immediate review. These include safety requirements, supervision and routine maintenance of construction works, as well as establishing an updated list of materials with properly set safety standards.
The committee would also review the supervision and accountability for the installation and operation of fire safety systems.
More broadly, it would assess systemic issues within the construction process, including potential collusion and conflicts of interest. It would examine the role and responsibilities of regulatory personnel across all stages of the project chain, including government departments and authorised professionals.
Corruption, bid-rigging and irregular tender practices within the building maintenance sector would be targeted, and the committee would also assess the adequacy of existing legislation and penalties to ensure proper accountability in all the areas under review.
The government was earlier asked why it had not chosen to set up a commission of inquiry instead.
The Chief Executive’s Office said in response that one purpose of establishing a committee rather than a commission was to expedite the process and enhance the efficiency of the investigation, including criminal probes.
A source told the Post earlier this month that it would be quicker to establish a review committee than a commission. The committee would also be able to operate more efficiently and receive direct instructions from Lee, who could sign an executive order to summon anyone needed and direct departments to provide evidence.
The committee would operate in a way similar to two earlier probes: one after a disastrous traffic accident in 2018 that killed 19, another in 2003 after a bus plunged 50 metres (164 feet) from an elevated road in Tuen Mun, killing 21.
Both an independent review committee and a commission of inquiry are independent bodies, typically chaired by a judge, that operate within terms of reference set by the executive branch. The primary difference lies in their investigative powers.
A commission of inquiry, which has been established five times since 1997 under the Commissions of Inquiry Ordinance to investigate matters of public importance, possesses more extensive investigative powers than an independent review committee.
Additional reporting by Fiona Chow

data.title: Why US review of Aukus submarine deal for Australia is kept secret
data.description:
The secretive aftermath of a United States review of the three-nation Aukus defence deal to supply Australia with nuclear-powered submarines is necessary to shield the pact from any doubts over its viability, which analysts say could undermine political and public support.
The Pentagon last Thursday announced it had completed its assessment of the project and found areas to put the deal on the “strongest possible footing”, citing US President Donald Trump’s bid to move the arrangement “full steam ahead”.
Set up in 2021, Aukus is a trilateral security partnership between Australia, the UK and the US to share advanced defence technology.
In June, the US launched a formal review into the deal primarily to ensure it aligns with the current administration’s “America First” agenda and to address concerns about Washington’s ability to meet its own submarine needs while supplying Canberra.
The review’s recommendations, however, had not been released and might never be made public, the Australian media reported.
At a meeting in Washington last Wednesday, Australian and American defence and foreign ministers reaffirmed in a joint statement their shared commitment to Aukus.

Australian Defence Minister Richard Marles said the Aukus plan to provide Canberra with nuclear-powered submarines in the next decade was on track, adding “the focus has to be on delivery”.
Manoj Harjani, a research fellow at the Singapore-based S Rajaratnam School of International Studies, said it was unsurprising that details of the review were not being released publicly.
This was also the case for reviews conducted by Australia and the UK, he noted.
“None of the three countries would want to publicly share any assessments that cast doubt on the viability of Aukus given its intended deterrence effects,” Harjani said, referring to increasing allied military presence and capabilities in the region to deter potential adversaries, mainly China.
The US review might also contain revised demands Australia had to fulfil to secure the delivery of the Virginia-class submarines, according to Harjani.
Nishank Motwani, a US-based senior fellow and director of alliance strategy at the Australian Strategic Policy Institute, said withholding the review could prevent the exposure of delays and delivery shortfalls, which could lead to a decline in support from politicians and the public for the submarine plan.
The bid to shield the plan from public scrutiny was “strategic”, Motwani said, noting that the three governments might only reveal more to align with a budget or capability announcement, or when they had a communication strategy to address any “potential backlash”.


“One explanation could be that the review has found that Aukus Pillar II has failed to deliver anything of substance and misallocated time, budget and resources while China continues to get stronger and fields more capabilities each day,” Motwani said.
While the main pillar is aimed at providing nuclear submarines to Australia, the advanced technology cooperation under pillar 2 focuses on joint development and sharing of emerging military tech like AI, cyber, quantum, hypersonic systems and electronic warfare.
Motwani said the technologies in pillar 2 had not been “prioritised, funded or answered the basic question of what problem they are meant to solve”.
“Credible deterrence demands fielding capabilities to complicate China’s decision-making calculus to use force by making the risk of failure too high. Given the stakes, it is imperative for Aukus to contribute to deterrence because without substance, it risks being nothing more than a label,” Motwani said.
Harjani said the key issue was meeting the commitment to deliver the submarines to Australia when there was already a significant backlog for the US Navy.
“Even with political will and more investment, it may be years before this backlog is resolved,” he warned.

Under Aukus, the US is committed to selling Australia three – with options for two more – Virginia-class submarines starting around 2032, with deliveries potentially extending into the mid-2030s, bridging the gap until a new Aukus-class submarine is ready.
This phased approach involves Australia first receiving American submarines, then co-developing a new indigenous SSN-Aukus design with the UK, using technology from all three countries.
Harjani said Aukus had survived changes in governments across all three partners.
“This is no small feat given the stakes involved, especially for Australia, which is bearing the largest burden among the three countries financially,” he said.
Canberra is expected to spend between A$268 billion (US$178 billion) and A$368 billion over the next 30 years, extending until the mid-2050s, representing its single largest ever defence investment.
Last October, Australia announced it would establish the Henderson Defence Precinct near Perth, committing A$12 billion to transform the existing Henderson shipyard into a base for building frigates and landing craft, and maintaining nuclear submarines.
That the announcement was made before the review was designed “to please Washington” to show that Australia is spending more on defence, according to Motwani.

This would also give Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese “political cover” that he did so independently and was not under pressure from Washington, Motwani said, making it harder for Australian hawks to claim that the Labor Party leader was “jeopardising” Aukus by not spending enough to keep the US committed to the deal.
“Even though the early stages of Henderson were announced when [US president Joe] Biden was in power, there is little doubt that Albanese calculated that his commitment to pour in billions in new funding would go far and keep Aukus afloat, making him a political winner at home,” Motwani said.

data.title: Differences should be ‘exception rather than norm’ for China and Philippines, Marcos says
data.description:
China’s new ambassador to the Philippines has said both sides should try to get relations back on a “healthy” course, while President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr said differences should be the “exception rather than the norm”.
Jing Quan said this would be essential for both countries’ development and beneficial to regional prosperity and progress when presenting his credentials to Marcos on Thursday, according to the Chinese embassy.
“China hopes the Philippines will proceed from its fundamental and long-term interests, work in the same direction with Beijing, and steer bilateral ties back onto a healthy and stable track as soon as possible,” he said.
Meanwhile, Marcos described China as one of the country’s “most important friends and partners”, according to the presidential office.
“I’m sure that there will be many opportunities for us to make the relationship between our two countries stronger, deeper and more important as the years go by,” he said.
“I look forward to working with you in managing the differences and intensifying the cooperation between our two countries. Our differences should be the exception in our ties rather than the norm.”
Tensions between the two countries have been escalating for more than a year with a series of confrontations in disputed areas of the South China Sea.
The Philippines is also the end point of the strategic “first island chain”, a strategically important series of archipelagos that stretches from Japan, and which could help the United States and its allies to restrict China’s access to the Pacific.
The Philippines is a long-standing US ally but under Marcos it has moved to strengthen its defensive ties with Washington, heightening China’s concerns.
On arrival in Manila on Saturday, Jing pledged to stabilise relations, even though it was “a heavy task”, according to an embassy social media account.
“As ambassador, I will firmly safeguard China’s national interests and dignity, while also serving as a bridge to ensure China-Philippines relations stabilise rather than deteriorate, and that our peoples draw closer instead of drifting apart,” Jing said.


Jing spent more than two decades focusing on the US in various roles and was previously deputy chief of mission at the embassy in Washington.
Manila appears to be looking for ways to balance the wish to assert Philippine claims in the resource-rich South China Sea while maintaining the key relationship with Beijing.
Two months ago, during the summit of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, Marcos said he wanted to invite Chinese President Xi Jinping to make his first visit to the Philippines since 2018. But he said this was conditional on “serious progress” being made on talks about a code of conduct for the South China Sea.
China and Asean have been discussing a code of conduct to manage tensions in the resource-rich waters – where China’s claims overlap with those of several members of the bloc – since the early 2000s.
In 2023 the two sides agreed to finalise the code by the middle of next year. The Philippines will chair Asean next year and Foreign Secretary Theresa Lazaro said recently that Manila would push to reach that goal, adding “if we need to have [talks] on a weekly basis, of course we will”.

data.title: Tensions rise as US prepares to seize more tankers with Venezuelan oil
data.description:
The United States is preparing to intercept more ships transporting Venezuelan oil following the seizure of a tanker this week, as it increases pressure on Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, six sources familiar with the matter said on Thursday.
The seizure was the first interdiction of an oil cargo or tanker from Venezuela, which has been under US sanctions since 2019. It came as the US executes a large-scale military build-up in the southern Caribbean and as US President Donald Trump pushes for Maduro’s ouster.
The latest US action has put shipowners, operators and maritime agencies involved in transporting Venezuelan crude on alert, with many reconsidering whether to sail from Venezuelan waters in the coming days as planned, shipping sources said.
Further direct interventions by the US were expected in the coming weeks targeting ships carrying Venezuelan oil that may also have transported oil from other countries targeted by US sanctions, such as Iran, according to the sources familiar with the matter who declined to be named due to the sensitivity of the issue.


Venezuela’s state oil company PDVSA did not reply to a request for comment. Venezuela’s government this week said the US seizure constituted a “theft”.
Asked whether the Trump administration planned further ship seizures, White House spokeswoman Karoline Leavitt told reporters she would not speak about future actions but said the US would continue executing the president’s sanctions policies.
“We’re not going to stand by and watch sanctioned vessels sail the seas with black market oil, the proceeds of which will fuel narcoterrorism of rogue and illegitimate regimes around the world,” she said.
The US has assembled a target list of several more sanctioned tankers for possible seizure, according to one of the people familiar with the matter.
The US Justice Department and Homeland Security had been planning the seizures for months, according to two of the people.
A reduction or halt in Venezuelan oil exports, the main generator of revenue for the Venezuelan government, would strain the Maduro government’s finances.

The US Treasury said on Thursday it imposed sanctions on six supertankers that, according to PDVSA’s internal documents and ship monitoring data, recently loaded crude in Venezuela, and on four Venezuelans, including three relatives of the country’s first lady, Cilia Flores. It was not known whether the newly sanctioned ships were among those now targeted for interception.
Wednesday’s seizure came after the US in recent months has carried out more than 20 strikes against what it says are drug vessels in the Caribbean and Pacific, killing more than 80 people. Experts have said that the strikes may be illegal extrajudicial attacks, while the US has said it is protecting Americans from drug cartels it has branded as terrorist organisations.
Further ship seizures could be aimed at tightening the financial screws on Maduro, according to a source briefed on US Venezuela policy. Maduro has alleged that the US military build-up was aimed at overthrowing him and gaining control of the Opec nation’s oil resources.
The new US tactic focuses on the activities of what is called the shadow fleet of tankers that transports sanctioned oil to China, the largest buyer of crude from Venezuela and Iran. A single vessel would often make separate runs on behalf of Iran, Venezuela and Russia, the sources added.
The seizure of the tanker, carrying the name Skipper, caused at least one shipper to temporarily suspend the voyages of three freshly loaded shipments totalling almost 6 million barrels of Venezuela’s flagship export grade, Merey, sources said.
“The cargoes were just loaded and were about to start sailing to Asia,” said a trading executive involved in dealing and shipping Venezuelan oil. “Now the voyages are cancelled and tankers are waiting off the Venezuelan coast as it’s safer to do that.”

US forces were monitoring tankers at sea and some vessels in Venezuelan ports, either being repaired or loaded, and waiting for them to sail into international waters before taking action, one of the sources said.
In the run-up to the seizure of Skipper, which was previously sanctioned for its oil trading with Iran, US forces had stepped up surveillance of waters close to Venezuela and neighbouring Guyana, another of the sources said.
At the White House, Leavitt said the seized vessel was expected to sail to a US port where the government intended to seize its cargo of oil through a formal legal process.
While the Venezuelan government described the US seizure as “an act of international piracy”, legal specialists said it did not fall under such a definition under international law.
“Because the capture was endorsed and sanctioned by the US, it cannot be considered piracy,” said Laurence Atkin-Teillet, a specialist on piracy and the law of the sea at Britain’s Nottingham Law School.
“The term piracy in this context appears to be rhetorical or figurative, rather than a legal usage.”

data.title: Scaffolding worker pay likely to rise in Hong Kong amid roll-out of new mesh
data.description:
The wages of scaffolding workers in Hong Kong are likely to increase as plastic meshes that have passed a new mandatory fire safety test are expected to be installed at more than 200 buildings from next month, according to a construction industry representative.
But another construction industry leader said the price of scaffolding nets would not rise significantly due to collective bulk procurement.
The remarks on Friday followed the government’s announcement a day before of tighter testing requirements for scaffolding nets in the wake of the deadly fire in Tai Po.
Under the rules, contractors must prove that the scaffolding mesh being used is fire-retardant, with samples to be tested in designated laboratories upon arrival in the city.
The mesh could only be installed after all the samples had passed lab tests, after the government had ordered scaffold netting to be removed at about 200 locations across Hong Kong in three days.
Substandard scaffolding mesh and foam boards were believed to have accelerated the spread of the fire at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po, claiming at least 160 lives, which was under renovation at the time.
The government said the first batch of nets would be installed “in a month”.
Chau Sze-kit, president of the Hong Kong Construction Industry Employees General Union, said the wages of the scaffolding workers would then be driven up when a number of mesh passed the newly required fire-retardant test and were ready to be installed.
He said wages were pushed up when authorities last Thursday ordered scaffold netting to be removed at about 200 locations across Hong Kong in three days.
“I think [the industry] will compete fiercely for manpower, as the wages were pushed up when the scaffolding net was required to be taken down in three days,” he said. “There was a situation when contractors just grabbed workers. I think a similar situation will happen again.”
He revealed that wages have increased in the three days last week and, up from HK$1,900 (US$244) to HK$2,100 to HK$2,700 daily, and even to HK$3,000 on Sunday.

The Development Bureau announced on Thursday a new mechanism for scaffold nets on external walls to be sampled on-site to ensure that those used at construction sites were up to standards. The new rule applies to all repair projects and new building works.
The new three-tier mechanism included requirements that contractors must provide certificates and test reports for the fire-retardant performance of the scaffold nets. They are also required to take a specified number of samples and send them to designated laboratories for testing and get passed before installation, while the government would conduct random checks after the scaffold nets were installed.
The government said the Construction Industry Council would offer interim central procurement of mesh, adding many contractors would opt for the service as the council’s bulk procurement process would speed up delivery.
Simon Liu Sing-pang, the president of the Hong Kong Construction Association, said he did not expect the price of scaffolding nets to rise significantly even though demand was surging from Hong Kong.
“I think the price of the scaffolding mesh would not rise too much as there would be collective bulk procurement and the cost could be controlled,” he said.
He expected the cost of testing would rise.
The government pledged on Thursday that it would seek more labs for testing.
The new arrangements came about two weeks after the fire at Wang Fuk Court killed at least 160 people and left nearly 5,000 homeless.
Earlier investigations found the fire, which broke out on November 26, began on bamboo scaffolding nets and spread rapidly across seven of the estate’s eight blocks, burning for 43 hours.
The mesh came under heavy scrutiny after investigators found substandard, non-flame retardant nets had been deliberately concealed at the estate during renovation.

data.title: Eastern boss Roberto Losada to take temporary charge of Hong Kong men’s football team
data.description:
Roberto Losada was named as the interim head coach of Hong Kong’s football team on Friday, taking over from Ashley Westwood who left by mutual consent last month.
The 49-year-old Losada will combine the post with his role in charge of Hong Kong Premier League side Eastern.
His first assignment will be the two-leg Guangdong-Hong Kong Cup on December 28 and January 3.
The Football Association of Hong Kong, China, announced the news in a release naming the squad for the cup fixtures and said the move was “to ensure stability during the transition period and maintain the team’s readiness for upcoming fixtures”.
“At the same time, the HKFA will officially launch an open recruitment process for the new head coach of the representative team,” the statement added.
Westwood’s final game in charge was the disappointing 2-1 loss at home to Singapore, which ended his side’s chances of qualifying for the Asian Cup finals in Saudi Arabia.
More to follow …

data.title: How to use coffee beans to flavour food and drinks, from ice cream and lamb loin to vodka
data.description:
Coffee is consumed most often as a drink, but it also makes for a distinctive seasoning.
The seeds of the coffee fruit (called “cherries”, because that is what they resemble) are picked when the fruit ripens to a deep red colour.
The pulp and skin are removed to reveal the green seed, which goes through several stages – including fermenting, washing, drying, hulling and grading – before the beans are stored or shipped.
The processed beans can be stored in their dried green state for several years, but for optimal flavour, once roasted and ground, they should be used as quickly as possible.

Everyone has their preferred method of brewing coffee. That can range from simply covering the ground beans with water, heating them together and then straining out the residue, to using elaborate (and expensive) machines that force water at high pressure through the ground coffee.
As a seasoning, the coffee – whether whole beans (for a more subtle flavour) or ground – can be infused in liquids other than water. You can make flavoured alcohol by dropping whole beans into a bottle of vodka; over time, the vodka will absorb the coffee flavour.
For ganache (to make truffles or for use as a cake filling), you can infuse ground coffee in hot cream (which enriches the texture), then strain out the grounds before whisking the liquid into chocolate. If you want more flavour, mix the chocolate with strong, hot coffee that has been brewed the regular way.
For ice cream, it is better to infuse ground beans in cream; if you were to make coffee the regular way, the water could affect the texture and make the ice cream icy rather than smooth and rich.

Coffee can also be used as a seasoning in savoury dishes.
Red-eye gravy is made by whisking brewed coffee (instead of the usual water or broth) into a roux that is made with flour and the drippings of pan-fried breakfast meats (such as bacon, ham or sausage).
In more modern preparations, lamb loin is coated in finely ground coffee beans, salt and other seasonings before being pan-fried, and oxtail is braised in a coffee-based broth.
This content first appeared in Post Magazine in 2014.

data.title: UK woman jailed after drunk e-scooter crash killed ‘beloved’ father in Australia
data.description:
A British backpacker has been sentenced to four years in prison after a fatal collision with a father-of-two while riding an electric scooter in Australia.
Alicia Kemp, 25, from Redditch, Worcestershire, appeared at Perth District Court, Western Australia, on Friday, where she was sentenced after pleading guilty to dangerous driving causing death while under the influence of alcohol.
Judge Wendy Hughes handed Kemp a four-year prison term, backdated to June 2025 for time served, with a non-parole period of two years.
She could be eligible for release in mid-2027.
Her driving licence has also been suspended for two years.
The woman was over Australia’s legal blood alcohol limit when she hit 51-year-old Thanh Phan in Perth on May 31.
The man died in hospital several days later, having suffered a brain bleed.
The backpacker, who pleaded guilty to the charge in August, and the e-scooter’s passenger were treated for minor injuries.
The tragedy brought e-scooter safety into the national spotlight in Australia, leading Perth and a number of surrounding councils to suspend hire services indefinitely.
In an earlier statement, the victim’s family described him as a “beloved husband, father of two and dear friend”.
Dangerous driving causing death carries a maximum sentence of 20 years’ imprisonment.
Under Western Australian law, e-scooter riders must wear a helmet, be sober, carry no passengers and be aged over 16.
